speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 30, 1995 with survivors Mark Laski, L-A-S-K-I. The interviewer is Claudia Steckler, S-T-E-C-K-L-E-R. The interview is taking in Boca Raton, near Miami, Florida, District USA. The language is English, and this is roll number 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 30, 1995. I'm interviewing Mark Laski. My name is Claudia Steckler, S-T-E-C-K-L-E-R. And we're in Boca Raton, Florida, USA. Please tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark, M-A-R-K, Laski-- spelled L-A-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 12, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, where you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bring me back to before the war for you. What is your childhood like? What were you like as a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: If it is all right with you, I'd like to throw in a little bit of a background on Poland at that time. I was born right one year after the end of the First World War, which ended in 1918 with the. Poland was a very unfortunate place to be born in, especially for a Jewish person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a period of brief independence for Poland-- from 1918, after the war ended, until 1939 when the other one started between Poland and Germany. Those were the 20 good years for me-- good in a sense that Poland was a free country at that time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to also mention [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fact this is the most tragic part of my childhood. My mother died about a week or two weeks after my birth as a result of the blood infection. 76 years ago, there was no penicillin. And there was no way of saving her. Frankly, [SIGHS] I only wish [INAUDIBLE] all the Nazis I should say to feel the-- feel the experience of not being able to say 'mother' forever. Because that's-- that is something I never could say.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, as a result of this tragedy, was nearly suicidal. He survived only because of the consolation he received from his sisters and brother. And he managed to somehow go on with life. I personally never stopped feeling guilty about it, because she died as a result of my birth. And that feeling of guilt still stays with me even today after all those years. Now I was raised by my father, [INAUDIBLE], and also by one of my aunts, Aunt Helen. She was truly the most wonderful person-- just as wonderful a person in my life as my father was. Nobody could do a better job at raising a child as my Aunt Helen. Unfortunately, they all perished. My father, and my aunts, and my cousins-- I never had any sisters or brothers-- and my uncles all perished in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you were brought up by your father and your aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like growing up in the house with them? What-- what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, my aunt did the best job she could possibly do. However, there is no substitute for a mother, or a mother's-- or for the child's feeling that he or she has a mother. There's simply no substitute for it. And I suffered a lot as a result of that, which is understandable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suffered later on as I got older. It made me even feel more guilty, because I had a better realization how terribly my father felt losing his wife. My mother was probably about 21 years old when she died. It was an experience which no words can really describe. My childhood was a result of this-- very difficult and very unhappy-- in spite of the fact that my father was trying his very best to kind of-- kind of compensate for the fact that I had no mother. Now in so far as my upbringing goes, I had a very secular kind of upbringing. Me, we were never religious. Even my grandfather was not religious. But we were very Jewish at the same time. And that may sound confusing to some people. But it is not confusing to me at all. Because I regard being Jewish as primarily as a nationality. My nationality is Jewish. Religion is somewhat very unimportant to me. I'm very much sensitive to ethical standards. This is-- ethics is for me and with me number one consideration, because that's the way I was raised practically. You know, you are very much a product of-- of what you were exposed as a child. As if we all know it or not, that's really so. And I-- my father was not religious, as I mentioned. And especially after losing his wife my father completely gave up faith in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: He could never answer the question why. Why would she be taken away from him at such a age? What did she do to deserve that? And he never overcame the pain. And I suffered doubly as a result of it-- one, you know, being motherless and the other one feeling for my father. As I got older, I better realized then, than when I was a little child, what my father had to go through. I had a better understanding of it. Were you and your father close then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. No son and father could be any closer than we ever were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it is difficult for a son to be objective. But to me, my father was the greatest person in the world in every way. He was extremely sensitive, very intelligent, very bright. He graduated from a school which I would call a Polish equivalent of MIT in Warsaw. Very interesting, by the way. The school was called the poly-technical, or you can call it an engineering school, which was founded by a couple of Russian Jews. But what they named Wawelberg and Rotwand-- they were the founders of this particular school. However, they were not-- so far as I know, they were born in Russia. But they-- their parents were, I think, German Jews. Because Wawelberg it's not a Russian name. Neither is Rotwand. But anyway, it was a very prominent school in Warsaw, in Poland-- an excellent school. In addition to being an electrical engineer, my father also went to Berlitz School of Languages. And he mastered English beautifully. He probably spoke English much better than most Americans do here. And he also knew French, and Russian, and Polish, of course. Now I must say something about Russia. I mentioned before to you that Poland was-- I don't know if you are aware of it, but Poland is in a very unfortunate geographical position being between Germany and Russia. And it was very often invaded by both of those countries. And as a result of the invasion, it was divided into German part of Poland occupied by the Germans and the Russian-- the Russians-- for the times of Czar. That was the times of the Czar, you know. And the southern part of Poland was occupied by the Austrians. That left a definite mark on the history of Poland, of course. And one of the reasons why my father was so fluent in Russian is because Warsaw, Warsaw, where we come from, and where he came from, was occupied by the Russians. So Russian was the dominate-- the dominating language, you know. And that was the-- that's the history of Poland is one almost continuous tragedy. So it definitely creates a life difficult for everybody in Poland. Now let me just make one comment on Jewish life in Poland. Now Poles, most of the Polish people, were anti-Semitism And that's an old story. Now what was responsible primarily for the anti-Semitism in Poland was the clergy, the church. The Catholic Church in Portland was brainwashing the people who believed that the Jews were murdered by Jesus Christ as many people believe even today, even in the United States. And that, alone, was the major contributing factor to anti-Semitism in Poland, you know. Most of the Poles are very devout Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to talk about what you saw as far as anti-Semitism in a little bit. But I want to get back to a little bit about when you were growing up in your home. I asked you what it was like. Was there a lot of music in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I'm sorry. I forgot it-- and how. I remember I was exposed to classical music so much. I practically grew up with Caruso and all the greatest singers. And we had a lot of records of the greatest symphonies and all that. My father played violin, by the way, as an amateur, and played beautifully. One of the-- one of the compositions that is always fresh in my mind, because I love it, number one, and because my father played it is Tchaikovsky's barcarolle called 'Barcarolle Melancolique.' In case you ever have a chance to listen to it, you may find it's a unique experience if you like music. And we Jewish people, most of us, have a special love for music I think-- not necessarily Jewish music. Music is, in my opinion, very international. I mean, there are many compositions written by many composers of all nationalities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's one thing-- as I was exposed a lot to music-- and languages. We are kind of born linguists. I forgot to mention it to you. Some people have a natural inclination to learn languages. [INAUDIBLE] Many Jewish people have that ability.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Europe, it is common that a person speaks two or three languages, because of the variety of countries, you know, neighboring here. [CLEARING THROAT] But in our family it was very typical. My father was a linguist, and he spoke beautiful English, and French, and Russian. And it's unfortunate that I didn't speak any English then. My-- all my English, whatever I know, is acquired in the United States during the 46 years I am here. I came without any knowledge of English at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other hobbies, other than the music?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like to think that I inherited a little bit of my father's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ability to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] do things which require some mechanical skills a little bit. He was an electrical engineer. But he was very good in-- in anything that has to do with mechanics and all that. And I had just a little bit of it, not much. What I will say that-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yeah, I shouldn't forget to mention to you, I had to go to several schools. Originally, we started with a Polish school, [INAUDIBLE]. When I say Polish, I mean it was a private school owned by some Polish institution, you know. But eventually, anti-Semitisim got so unbearable that my father took me out from that school, and I had to go-- I went to Jewish school. Now when I say Jewish school, I don't mean that it was teaching just Jewish subjects, or had anything to do with Jewish religion at all. No, unfortunately I wish it had. But it didn't. It was Jewish school, because it was owned by a group of Jewish teachers. It was a private, as I said, gymnasium as I called it. Not that I called it that. That's the way it was called.

speaker: interviewer
text: A gymnasium would be something halfway between our high school here and university. In other words, it is definitely much more than high school. But it's not quite a university-- you know, like a community college maybe you would call it? Something of that sort. It was an excellent school. And after the school, once you graduated from that private gymnasium, as I said, you are qualified to go to the university.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, when the war broke out in 1939 between Poland and Germany on September 1st, so it completely destroyed all my dreams and my plans. I was planning on studying in university. I was two years studying psychology in Warsaw. But my ultimate dream was-- and maybe it's typically Jewish-- I don't know-- become a doctor, which never happened. And I wanted to go to-- to Sorbonne in France, to Paris. But the war broke out, and that destroyed everything completely. That was September 1st, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to the war starting, do you remember what your address was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Like I said, Warsaw. Now the name of the street was Wielka, spelled W-I-E-L-K-A, Wielka-- which means 'grand,' like Grand Street-- street. And the number was 19-- 1, 9.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember celebrating Jewish holidays at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, we were kind of the traditional family-- like, you know, Passover, Rosh Hashanah. We-- we like the tradition very much. That-- that we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you bar mitzvah'd?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was bar mitzvah'd. But we didn't have anything like a wedding over here. Nothing like that ever existed in Europe at all. That's an American invention. [COUGHING] Bar mitzvah was strictly for the few people, the family. Usually the boy would receive a watch, which was a major event. But it was not a wedding like here, my god.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about your father. What are your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father's names were-- in Polish, it is Izak-- Isaac in English, I-S-A-A-C. My mother's name-- my mother's maiden name was Rochman-- R-O-C-H-M-A-N. And her first name was Rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an electrical engineer. That doesn't necessarily mean that he always was able to find a job, because with the anti-Semitisim in Poland and all that, it wasn't easy for a Jewish person to get a position in engineering-- in spite of the fact that you had a degree and you might have been the best expert in the field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider yourself middle class? Or upper middle class? Or lower--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not upper middle class at all. Under the best of circumstances, maybe middle class. We had some tough times quite often. Although the period, I must mention, that the period after the First World War, which is, let's say, 1918 until 1939, was considered a period of lot of relative prosperity in Poland. Because Poland was in the war, destroys a lot. And then is a period of rebuilding, which creates a lot of jobs, and industries, and stuff. But things where pretty rough in general-- especially for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before I get to that, you mentioned your schooling. Did you have anyone that was particularly your best friend, or a group of friends that you hung around with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I have-- I have some schoolmates-- friends, yes. But they're all gone. Oh, you mean the names? Yeah, there was, as a matter of fact, this man was here, in the United States. His name was Segel, Mietek. He had my first name, which is Mieczeslaw, or Mietek in Polish. I was called-- and that was my original name, Mietek, spelled M-I-E-T-E-K. M-I-E-T-E-K. That was the, like you can say Bill and William is the same name really. But Mietek was like a Bill. But my official name was Mieczeslaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you want me to give you the Polish spellings of it? It may be interesting to you. It's quite complicated for anybody who was not born in Poland. M- as in Mary, I-E-C- as in Charles, Z- as in zebra, E-S-L-A-W. Mieczeslaw. If you've head of Mieczyslaw Rostropovich, the famous Russian cellist musician.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that is an equivalent of the Russian name Mieczyslaw. In Polish, it is Mieczeslaw. There is a great resemblance between Polish and Russian. Both of those languages belong to the Slavic group. So many words are pretty much the same, except they are pronounced differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain friends with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a number of years, maybe four or five years, something like that. Then was another fellow by the name Mirlas, M-I-R-L-A-S. It was friend of mine also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you lose touch with him during the war or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we lost contact completely. I must make one very crucial here give you very crucial information. As I mentioned to you-- and you know that September 1st was the day between-- when the war started between Germany and Poland. However, I was in Warsaw until September 6. Poland was destroyed practically in a couple of weeks. It took us a couple days to prepare ourselves for some sort of an exodus. Now my father was very much concerned with my life, because the young people-- I wasn't even 20 years old at the time. I was 20 years minus 12 days, because the war started on the 1st, and my birthday's September 12. So my father wanted me to get out from Warsaw as quick as possible to save my life. And on the six days-- sixth day of Warsaw, hundreds of thousands-- sixth day of-- sixth day of war, sorry-- hundreds of thousands of Jews were running away from Warsaw. The Germans were approaching already Warsaw, very close, and trying-- trying to go away as far as possible to the eastern part of Poland. Because, you know, Germany was on the western [? part-- ?] the western side of Poland. They were approaching to Warsaw. So all of us were trying to get away, ran away from. So you could see thousands-- hundreds of thousands of people marching, towards the eastern border of between Poland and Russia. Now this was another day which I will never forget. We were approached-- we approached a town in this march towards the east-- eastern part of Poland. There was a town under a River Bug, B-U-G-- a town by the name Malkinia, M-A-L-K-I-N-I-A. And-- and that town-- that town was like a borderline between the Russians and the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the Russians wouldn't let us in. They were screaming, you know, hollering at us in Russian [NON-ENGLISH], which means go back, go back. Who wanted to go back? Going back is like going to commit suicide. But they didn't care. So we were sitting there, and it was already cold-- almost October, something like that. And it began to snow. And Poland is not the climate like here. And it was-- and they were with dogs, and with bayonets and with rifles pushing us back. They weren't shooting, but they wanted us to go back, not to cross that borderline. They wouldn't let us inside Russia, which was considered at that time the only way to save our lives. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] finally, finally, they received some orders to let some of us through. And then we found ourselves in the eastern part of Poland, which was kind of occupied by Russian. Now what happened-- nobody knew about it-- there was a secret agreement between Hitler, between Germany, and Russia to divide Poland, which is a repetition of the old tradition, so far as Germany's, Russia is concerned. And they divided Poland into a German occupation zone and a Russian occupation zone. So we, fortunately, found-- found ourselves then in the part of Poland which is occupied by the Russians. That was a time of Stalin. It was bad-- very bad. But nothing compared to, of course, with the situation in Poland occupied by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now being there-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't remember all the details. But I know that-- yeah, we could not remain there too long. The Russian, or the communists said that us coming from Warsaw is kind of a suspicious thing for them, because they said we never know. You could be a spy for the Nazis, or a spy for the Germans-- you know, nonsense like this. And I was trying to convince them that we are Jewish, and we running away from the Nazis. Why would we-- why would we be spying for the Nazis? But you couldn't convince them of it. So anyway, they told us you have to go somewhere else. You can't be in that-- in that-- how do we call this-- border zone, you know, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remember being in Rovno, which is also in the eastern part of Poland-- a little town, Rovno. And I was trying to get a job. I couldn't get a job. It was a miserable life, terrible. It was almost starvation. And then eventually I was trying to get-- oh, yeah. I was trying to get to see some of my distant family in the-- [HORN HONKING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, in [NON-ENGLISH], which is a little town near Lvov. Maybe you've heard of Lemberg, Lvov-- kind of a capital of the Polish part if Ukraine. I must tell you before I go any further, and I don't want to confuse you about it. Ukraine and Belarus are border areas of Poland and Russia. Part of both of those countries, Ukraine, is between the Polish borders, and part is part of Russia. And the same story is today. Part of Ukraine is with Poland, part is-- so we wanted to go to this city, [NON-ENGLISH], which is close to Lvov, which is in Ukraine actually-- Polish part of Ukraine. Why you go? Because we had some distant families there, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way to Lvov-- please listen to this carefully-- on the way to Lvov, there is a little town called [NON-ENGLISH] I'm confused. I'm sorry. Forget about Lvov. On the way to [NON-ENGLISH], and I told you I have some distant family, [NON-ENGLISH], There is a little town called [NON-ENGLISH]. You could not get to [NON-ENGLISH] if you don't pass [NON-ENGLISH]. In that town of [NON-ENGLISH] we heard shooting still going on between the Russian-- between the Germans and the Poles. The war was still going on there. And one day, I see no Pole, no shooting anymore. I heard no shooting anymore. And I see German tanks there. And that was they occupied already [INAUDIBLE]. Obviously they won the war in no time. It was what they call a Blitzkrieg, which means lightning speed, krieg-- war. Krieg is war. In German, it's krieg. And I was with Germans there two days with the Nazis in [NON-ENGLISH]. First thing they did, they-- ordered all the Jews to assemble in the marketplace in [NON-ENGLISH]. And they gave us an incredible job. They gave me a pile of about a thousand dishes to wash-- you know, the dishes for the army-- cold water and sand. You know, soap, no nothing. And I couldn't lift that stack. It was a huge stack, and I was hungry. So they hit me, and almost killed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father was still in Warsaw. Because, you see, I had a hope to remain-- return towards--

speaker: interviewer
text: Three minutes to wrap up what you were saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I continue?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just wind up what you were saying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You were telling me about your father and-- and just wind up what you were saying about your father being--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he remained in Warsaw, because he could not liquidate everything so quickly, you know. He had some business deals there. And he was hoping to see me in-- in eastern part of Poland a little bit later. He was hoping to be able to be able to make the same trip I did, not knowing that this was impossible to do with, you know, no knowledge of that.
